elements.sent-2411-001: In this study, we define the maze as a network with vertices connected by unweighted edges.
elements.sent-2411-002: Our analysis of the stationary state of the Grover walk on finite graphs, where we strategically place absorbing holes and self-loops on specific vertices, demonstrates that this approach can effectively solve mazes.
elements.sent-2411-003: By setting arbitrary start and goal vertices in the underlying graph, we obtain the following long-time results: (i) in tree structures, the probability amplitude is concentrated exclusively along the shortest path between start and goal; (ii) in ladder-like structures with additional paths, the probability amplitude is maximized near the shortest path.
elements.sent-2411-004: The development of algorithms for finding the shortest path in networks has a rich history~\cite{network_rev1,network_rev2 and plays an important role in a wide array of practical applications in our daily lives, such as in car navigation systems.}
elements.sent-2411-005: The term 'networks' includes various forms, from physical structures like roads and power lines to more abstract ideas.
elements.sent-2411-006: From the study of networks, a larger field known as complex networks has emerged~\cite{complex1,complex2 , which has recently expanded its focus to include quantum mechanics~\cite{complex_q .}}
elements.sent-2411-007: The maze-solving problem can be viewed as a fundamental case of the shortest path problem in networks.
elements.sent-2411-008: Here, we define a maze as a network consisting of unweighted edges that represent distance parameters.
elements.sent-2411-009: Consequently, maze-solving techniques are regarded as a subset of the shortest path problem, with various algorithms, including breadth-first search~\cite{book1 , Dijkstra's algorithm~\cite{dijkstra , and the Bellman–Ford algorithm~\cite{Bellman , having been developed in classical settings.}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: The challenge of solving mazes has become an important area of research in the field of natural computing.
elements.sent-2411-011: Various natural phenomena, such as slime molds~\cite{NYT,TKN , the Belousov–Zhabotinsky (BZ) reaction~\cite{BZ , electrical discharges~\cite{discharge , and the propagation of light in waveguides~\cite{photonic , have mechanisms that can effectively navigate mazes.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: While humans typically rely on intelligence to solve such problems, nature's ability to accomplish the same task highlights that intelligence is not strictly necessary; instead, the key lies in algorithms governed by simple rules.
elements.sent-2411-013: In exploring networks like mazes, the movement of a probabilistic walker throughout the network represents a specific type of algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-014: In classical exploration methods, the walker corresponds to probability density, while in quantum exploration methods, it relates to probability amplitude.
elements.sent-2411-015: The basic approach to classical exploration is the random walk.
elements.sent-2411-016: Quantum walks have been introduced as the quantum version of random walks and are expected to improve effectiveness in exploration problems because their probability propagation speed is directly proportional to time, unlike random walks, where it is proportional to the square root of time~\cite{FH,Am1 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Furthermore, quantum walks can utilize the interference of probability amplitudes, providing another advantage in exploration tasks.
elements.sent-2411-018: The maze-solving approach utilizing quantum walks is derived from quantum search~\cite{Search_K,Search_C,search_A,Portugal .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Investigating specific points in a network using quantum walks is similar to implementing Grover's algorithm on structured networks~\cite{Grover .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Koch et al. extended their research on identifying structural anomalies in networks~\cite{anoma1,anoma2,anoma3 and demonstrated that executing a Grover walk in a maze—where the vertices at the start and goal undergo phase inversion—can result in the temporary identification of the shortest path~\cite{Koch2017,Koch2018,Koch2019,Hillery2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: In contrast, Matsuoka et al. advanced the study of quantum walks by accounting for both inflow and outflow~\cite{FelHil1,FelHil2,HS,HSS1,HS2,KSS,SKKS , creating a more robust and versatile approach~\cite{MYLS .}}
elements.sent-2411-022: In our paper, by integrating self-loop structures at the maze's start and goal, along with incorporating holes that absorb probability amplitudes, we introduce the {\it navigation vector which is induced by the fundamental cycles and the shortest path between the pair of the vertices having the self-loop of the underlying graph.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Our mathematical results tells us that a quantum walker takes a time to find autonomously the navigation vector, and as a result, it clings along the shortest path between the start and goal of some mazes in the stationary state.
elements.sent-2411-024: Although Matsuoka et al.'s method is not suitable for mazes with cycles that have an odd number of nodes, it has been shown to extend to tree structures and ladder configurations featuring multiple paths.
elements.sent-2411-025: Furthermore, since all time evolution can be expressed as the problem of matrix exponentiation defined by the network structure, this approach enables efficient computations using GPUs, independent of the number of steps or vertices, suggesting its potential as a classical algorithm inspired by quantum mechanics.
elements.sent-2411-026: In this paper, we present a mathematical reconstruction of the maze-solving method utilizing Grover walk, leading to an explicit derivation of its limit distribution.
elements.sent-2411-027: In Section 2, we outline the detailed structure of the graph that represents the maze and discuss the time evolution of the Grover walk.
elements.sent-2411-028: We also examine the spectral properties of the Grover walk in this context, demonstrating that the probability distribution in the long-time limit is determined by the overlap between the initial state and the eigenspace associated with the eigenvalue $-1$, known as the navigation vector.
elements.sent-2411-029: In Section 3, we detail the limit distribution of the Grover walk specifically for tree structures and ladder graphs.
elements.section-2411-002: Settings and properties of Grover walk
elements.sent-2411-030: Let $G_o = (V_o,A_o)$ be a finite connected and symmetric digraph such that an arc $a \in A_o$ if and only if its inverse arc $\bar{a}\in A_o$.
elements.sent-2411-031: The origin and terminal vertices of $a \in A_o$ are denoted by $o(a) \in V_o$ and $t(a) \in V_o$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-032: We assume that $G_o$ has no multiple arcs and self-loops.
elements.sent-2411-033: This symmetric digraph $G_o$ is called the {\it underlying graph .}
elements.sent-2411-034: In this paper, we consider the problem of solving a maze by using a quantum walk.
elements.sent-2411-035: The maze is regarded as the underlying graph $G_o$.
elements.sent-2411-036: To let the quantum walk solve the maze, we slightly deform the underlying graph as follows.
elements.sent-2411-037: The start and goal of the maze can be placed at any vertex in $V_o$, and they are indexed by $s$ and $g$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-038: To find a route from $s$ to $g$, we add one self-loop each to vertices $s$ and $g$, which are also indexed by $s$ and $g$.
elements.sent-2411-039: We also add a vertex which is called sink and indexed by $d$.
elements.sent-2411-040: The sink is connected only to the start vertex $s$.
elements.sent-2411-041: The arc which connects $s$ and $d$ is also indexed by $d$, where $o(d) =s$ and $t(d) =d$.
elements.sent-2411-042: In the following, the graph induced by the underlying graph $G_o$ is denoted by $G=(V,A)$; the digraph $G$ contains these self-loops, the sink vertex and arcs connecting to the start and the sink vertices.
elements.sent-2411-043: The degree of $v \in V$ is given by \begin{equation}{\rm deg}(v) = |\{ a\in A \, | \, t(a)=v \}|.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-044: By the symmetry of the digraph, the equation ${\rm deg (v) = |\{ a\in A \, | \, o(a)=v \}|$ holds.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{center} \includegraphics[bb=0 0 600 270, scale=0.4]{maze1.png} \vspace*{-0.3cm} \caption{Setup of the start and goal with self-loops and a sink.}\label{fig1} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-046: We use the Grover walk $U$ on ${\mathcal H _A$ defined by \begin{equation}(U\xi)(a) = -\xi(\bar{a}) + \frac{2}{{\rm deg}(o(a))} \sum_{t(b)=o(a)} \xi (b)\end{equation} for any $\xi \in {\mathcal H _A$.}}
elements.sent-2411-048: We want to assume that the quantum walker at the sink goes away and never comes back.
elements.sent-2411-049: To represent this, we use the projection $P$ given by \begin{equation}\label{projP} (P \xi)(a) = \begin{cases} \xi(a) a \neq d,\bar{d} 0 a= d, \bar{d}\end{cases}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-050: For $n=0,1,2,\ldots$, the $n$-th iteration of the Grover walk with the sink is defined by \begin{equation}\zeta_n (a) = \left( (PU)^n \zeta \right) (a) \quad (a \in A).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-051: Then, the finding probability at vertex $v \in V\backslash \{d\}$ and at time $n$ can be defined by \begin{equation}\mu_n(v) = \sum_{t(a)=v} |\zeta_n(a)|^2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-052: Remark that our assumption is also represented by using an infinite dimensional environmental Hilbert space ${\mathcal H _E$ and an appropriately extended unitary $\tilde{U}$ on ${\mathcal H _A \oplus {\mathcal H _E$ \cite{KSS .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: However, since \begin{equation}(\tilde{U}^n \tilde{\zeta})(a) = \left( (PU)^n \zeta \right) (a)\end{equation} for any $a \in A\backslash \{d, \bar{d}\}$, we will adopt the RHS represented by the finite system in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-054: Here $\tilde{\zeta}$ is the extension of the initial state $\zeta\in \mathcal{H _A$ to $\mathcal{H _{A}\oplus \mathcal{H _E$.}}}
elements.subsection-2411-002: Spectral decomposition of $U$ for maze solving
elements.sent-2411-055: To solve the maze, it is important to know the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of the Grover walk $U$.
elements.sent-2411-056: In particular, we focus on the eigenvectors of $U$ which are invariant under the action of the projection $P$; as we will see, the contribution of the eigenvectors which are not invariant under the action of $P$ reduces exponentially to $0$ as the time step goes to infinity.

elements.sent-2411-058: Remark that the eigenvalue $\lambda$ of $U$ satisfies $|\lambda|=1$.
elements.sent-2411-059: Let $\xi \in {\mathcal H _A$ be an eigenvector of $U$ associated with the eigenvalue $\lambda$.}

elements.subsection-2411-004: The case the graph has cycles

elements.subsection-2411-005: Comparison with the digraph with a sink at the goal
elements.sent-2411-215: A method of maze-solving was considered by adding the sink at the goal in \cite{MYLS .}
elements.sent-2411-216: In this subsection, we discuss differences between the method in \cite{MYLS and ours.}
elements.sent-2411-217: The digraph in the left hand side of Figure {\rm \ref{fig3 is considered in \cite{MYLS .}}}
elements.sent-2411-218: When we put the sink at the goal, the state $(PU)^n \zeta$ does not converge.
elements.sent-2411-219: The reason why this happens is that there exists an eigenvector in {\rm (1) whose eigenvalue is not $-1$.}
elements.sent-2411-221: Then, the vector defined as in the right hand side of Figure {\rm \ref{fig3 is the eigenvector associated with the eigenvalue $\lambda$.}}
elements.sent-2411-222: This eigenvector is the cause of oscillations of amplitudes (See \cite{KSS, MYLS ).}
elements.figure-2411-005: \begin{center} \includegraphics[bb=0 0 820 400, scale=0.4]{maze3.png} \vspace*{-0.3cm} \caption{Digraph considered in \cite{MYLS} and an eigenvector in (1).}\label{fig3} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-223: On the other hand, when we put the sink at the start, the state $(-1)^n (PU)^n \zeta$ definitely converges.
elements.sent-2411-224: When the sink is at the goal, we can make an example where the amplitudes of the state $(PU)^n \zeta$ appear at points other than the start-to-goal path.
elements.sent-2411-226: Then, the vector defined as in Figure \ref{fig4 is in {\rm (1) whose eigenvalue is not $-1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-227: Therefore, $(PU)^n \zeta$ always has non-zero coefficients on the cycle of length $k$.
elements.sent-2411-228: This means that the position of the quantum walker does not tells us the shortest route.
elements.sent-2411-230: We have established a mathematical framework for maze solving using quantum walks and explicitly derived the limit distribution.
elements.sent-2411-231: Our method resembles the placement of food at the start and goal in slime mold maze-solving by incorporating self-loops at these vertices, which enables the emergence of the shortest path within the quantum walk structure.
elements.sent-2411-232: Moreover, we found that adding the sink at the start rather than at the goal effectively prevents the emergence of unstable states.
elements.sent-2411-233: These phenomena are based on the spectral properties of the Grover walk, characterized by a birth space defined by fundamental cycles, self-loops, and sinks.
elements.sent-2411-234: Although this eigenspace has not been utilized in existing quantum search algorithms, it plays an important role in our maze-solving methodology.
elements.sent-2411-235: We showed that Grover walk, which is a typical model for the discrete-time quantum walk, has the property choosing the shortest path between the start and goal autonomously in some special setting where there are many detours.
elements.sent-2411-236: We found that the feeds on the start and goal in the slime molds computing correspond to the self loops in the Grover walk maze solving.
elements.sent-2411-237: This phenomenon is based on the spectral property of the Grover walk having the {\it birth space which is characterized by the fundamental cycles, self-loops and the sink.}
elements.sent-2411-238: This eigenspace was wasted in the quantum search algorithms, however it plays the main role in the maze solving.. \fi.
